nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still surface waters: streams, canals, rice paddies, ponds, and coastal estuaries; dense marginal or floating vegetation; often in brackish tidal inlets as well as freshwater

decorPreferences: floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, salvinia) to provide surface cover and reduce jumping
tight-fitting lid — essential given high escape risk
gentle surface flow or still water
emergent or overhanging vegetation for security

diet.notes: Obligate surface-insectivore in the wild; takes prey from the water surface and just below. Prefers live or frozen small invertebrates: fruit flies (Drosophila), small crickets, mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Many specimens initially refuse dry foods; small floating pellets or micro-sticks can be accepted after conditioning. Feed 2–3 small portions daily and remove uneaten live food. Avoid sinking foods — this species rarely feeds below the surface film.

goodWith: peaceful mid- and bottom-dwelling fish of similar size (corydoras, small rasboras, ember tetras)
small gouramis that do not compete at the surface
other halfbeaks in a well-sized tank with visual breaks
avoidWith: other male Wrestling Halfbeaks in small tanks (jaw-wrestling and fin damage)
fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — long lower jaw is vulnerable
large or aggressive fish that cause chronic stress
boisterous surface feeders that out-compete for food (hatchetfish in large numbers)
Celebes Halfbeak (Nomorhamphus liemi, fw-fish-294) — different genus and care requirements; mixing invites confusion and potential aggression

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Dermogenys pusilla. Check FishBase for current URL and attribution before use.

summary: The Wrestling Halfbeak is a slender, surface-dwelling Southeast Asian livebearer named for its elongated lower jaw. Males famously interlock jaws in wrestling bouts — a practice historically exploited as a fighting fish. An obligate surface-insectivore requiring live or frozen foods and a tightly-lidded tank.
careNotes:
Dermogenys pusilla is a slender viviparous halfbeak found across the slow-moving lowland waters of Southeast Asia, from Thailand to the Indonesian archipelago. It inhabits the surface film almost exclusively, and tanks should reflect this: ample floating plant cover (frogbit, salvinia, water lettuce) provides security and breaks line-of-sight between rival males, while also crucially reducing jumping. A tightly-fitting lid is non-negotiable — halfbeaks are persistent jumpers and will find any gap. A tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint is the practical minimum; larger tanks significantly reduce male–male aggression. Water should be warm (24–28°C), neutral to slightly alkaline (pH 7.0–8.0), and moderately hard. A small amount of marine salt (SG 1.001–1.003) is sometimes added to recreate the brackish coastal conditions of some wild populations, and can help reduce disease incidence, but is not mandatory.

The most critical aspect of care is feeding: this species is an obligate surface-insectivore and many specimens reject dry foods entirely on arrival. A diet of live and frozen small invertebrates — Drosophila fruit flies, mosquito larvae, daphnia, small brine shrimp, and bloodworms — should be offered from the outset. Some individuals can be trained to accept small high-quality floating pellets over time, but a live-food supplement should always be maintained. Offer food at the surface; sinking foods are ignored. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

Male–male aggression centres on jaw-wrestling: males interlock their elongated lower mandibles and push against each other, a behaviour that historically was exploited in parts of Southeast Asia as an analogue to fighting fish contests. In a well-decorated, spacious tank a single male : two-to-three female ratio is recommended. Multiple males can coexist if the tank is large enough and sight-lines are broken with floating vegetation. The extended lower jaw is delicate and should not be confused with the lower jaw of the Celebes Halfbeak (Nomorhamphus liemi, fw-fish-294), which is a distinct viviparous genus endemic to Sulawesi with somewhat different care requirements.
breedingNotes: Dermogenys pusilla is viviparous (a true livebearer), bearing fully-formed fry after a gestation period of approximately 4–6 weeks. Broods are typically small — 5–25 fry — but females can produce consecutive broods. Fry are born relatively large at 8–10 mm and are capable of taking micro-live foods (newborn brine shrimp, micro-worms, Drosophila larvae) from birth. Parental predation of fry is possible; dense floating plant cover or a separate breeding/fry-rearing tank substantially improves survival. Sexing is straightforward: males are smaller and slimmer with a modified anal fin (andropodium) used for internal fertilisation; females are larger-bodied with a darkened gravid spot. A 1:2 to 1:3 male:female ratio helps reduce male harassment of gravid females. Conditioning on live foods (mosquito larvae, daphnia, fruit flies) before and during breeding improves fry yield.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dermogenys pusilla
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (SE Asia: Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Myanmar, Vietnam, Philippines), water parameters (temperature 22–28°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 90–268 ppm / ~5–15 dH), adult size (males to 4 cm, females to 7 cm), habitat (surface of slow/still waters, often in brackish conditions), diet (surface insects, small invertebrates), breeding notes (viviparous, 5–25 fry, 4–6 week gestation), and male aggression/jaw-wrestling behaviour. Taxonomy: Zenarchopteridae, Beloniformes confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Dermogenys pusilla
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Beloniformes / Zenarchopteridae), adult size (to 8.5 cm TL recorded but typical aquarium specimens 5–7 cm), native range (peninsular SE Asia, Malay Archipelago), habitat (freshwater and brackish coastal lowland streams), and diet (surface invertebrates). adultSizeCm set to 4–7 cm per SeriouslyFish sex-differentiated max; FishBase TL maximum of 8.5 cm represents large wild females.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Halfbeak Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care summary. Confirmed surface-insectivore feeding behaviour, high escape risk, lid requirement, floating plant recommendations, and brackish salt tolerance. Cited 10-gallon (38 L) minimum for small groups; 60 L used in this file as a more robust minimum per SeriouslyFish guidance and male aggression considerations.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Halfbeak profile
additional.notes: Supplementary data: confirmed jaw-wrestling behaviour and historical use as fighting fish in SE Asia, care level intermediate, live food dependency, viviparous reproduction, and distinction from Nomorhamphus (Celebes Halfbeak). Temperature range 24–28°C and brackish tolerance also noted.

additional.name: FishBase — Nomorhamphus liemi (Celebes Halfbeak, fw-fish-294)
additional.notes: Reference cross-check to distinguish D. pusilla from the Celebes Halfbeak (Nomorhamphus liemi). Nomorhamphus liemi is endemic to Sulawesi, belongs to a separate genus within Zenarchopteridae, and has slightly different care requirements (cooler water, 22–26°C). Cited in avoidWith and careNotes to disambiguate the two species sold under similar names.

fish.breedingNotes: Viviparous livebearer; gestation ~4–6 weeks, 5–25 fry per brood. Fry born at ~8–10 mm and can accept newborn brine shrimp from birth. Condition breeders on live foods. Floating plant cover or a separate fry tank needed to protect against parental predation. Keep at 1:2–1:3 male:female ratio.
